items.title: The prompt set is built from category+city, and is empty when either is missing
items.precondition: Pure builder, no network or DB.
steps: Build a prompt set for category 'endodontist' + city 'Austin, TX'.
Build with an empty category, and with a whitespace-only city.
items.expected: Two prompts (generic + contextualized) carrying the category and city; an empty set when either input is missing, so the runner skips instead of asking a meaningless question.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — 2 buildPromptSet cases passed in the 83-test A3 run and the 539-test full suite (Node 22).

items.title: Detector: mentioned + position when the practice name appears
items.precondition: Pure detector over a fixture answer. No network or DB.
steps: Detect over an answer listing the practice name.
Detect over an answer naming only other practices.
items.expected: mentioned=true with the raw 1-based line index; mentioned=false when absent. position is diagnostic only and is never surfaced as a rank.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — 2 detectAppearance cases passed.
items.notes: A log, never a score. No rank is derived from position.

items.title: Detector: cited=true only when a citation's real HOST is the practice's own
items.precondition: Pure detector over structured citation fixtures.
steps: Detect with the practice's host as the real hostname of a citation URL.
Detect with the practice's domain present ONLY in a third party's citation title prose.
Detect with the name in prose but only third-party citations.
items.expected: cited=true only when a PARSED citation URL's hostname is the practice's own (or a subdomain), and citedSource stores the field that proved it. A domain sitting in a third party's title prose proves nothing and records nothing. A bare name match never implies a citation.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — host-match, non-canonical-title, and ambiguity-guard cases passed in the 83-test A3 run and the 539-test full suite (Node 22).
items.notes: CHANGED IN ROUND 4. The previous version of this test asserted the FABRICATION: a citation {url:null, title:'Bright Smiles — brightsmiles.com'} was expected to record cited=true. The test encoded the bug, so it was corrected, not preserved.

items.title: Detector anti-fabrication: no positive from a lookalike, prose, ccTLD, or longer name
items.precondition: Pure detector over adversary fixtures.
steps: Detect against a competitor superstring domain (bestsmiledental.com vs smiledental.com).
Detect against a domain appearing only in prose.
Detect against a different ccTLD host, a longer competitor name, and a common-word name inside a longer name.
Detect against a real subdomain of the practice's own domain.
items.expected: Every lookalike is rejected; only the genuine subdomain citation counts. The detector prefers a MISSED positive over a FABRICATED one.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — 6 anti-fabrication regression cases passed in the 83-test A3 run and the 539-test full suite (Node 22).
items.notes: Binding honesty rule (spec + research/aeo-measurement-spec.md §3): never record a mention/citation that isn't genuinely the practice.

items.title: Registry runs only CONFIGURED engines
items.precondition: Inject one configured and one unconfigured fake adapter.
steps: Set adapters to a configured gemini and an unconfigured perplexity.
Read the configured set.
items.expected: Only the configured engine is returned; an engine with no key is never run half-built.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — registry case passed.

items.title: SerpApi adapter parses the REAL ai_overview shape and keeps each reference's link
items.precondition: fetch is stubbed with a live-shaped SerpApi google_ai_overview payload: text_blocks plus references as {title, link, snippet, source, index}.
steps: Stub SERPAPI_API_KEY and fetch with the live-shaped payload.
Query the adapter and inspect the returned citations.
items.expected: Both the link and the title survive as a structured citation; the URL is never discarded in favour of the title.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — SerpApi parse case passed. MUTATION-VERIFIED: restoring the old title-preferred-over-link mapping fails this test and T7 and nothing else, so the test catches the real defect rather than restating the code.
items.notes: Dave review #165 round 2, item 2. HONEST LIMIT: fetch is stubbed, so this proves the PARSE against the documented/real response shape, NOT the network. The live call is T19.

items.title: A normal {title, link} reference is detected as CITED end-to-end
items.precondition: Stubbed live-shaped SerpApi payload whose reference title carries no domain and whose link is the practice's own site.
steps: Query the adapter for the live-shaped payload.
Run the detector over the result with the practice identity.
items.expected: cited=true, and citedSource is the practice URL. Previously the adapter kept only the title — a headline with no hostname — so a real citation of the practice's own site recorded cited=false and under-reported visibility.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — end-to-end cited case passed. Mutation-verified alongside T6.
items.notes: Dave review #165 round 2, item 2. This is the adapter->detector contract the finding named: the adapter must not decide which field is useful, because the detector needs a hostname.

items.title: SerpApi adapter tolerates partial shapes and fails loudly on a non-ok response
items.precondition: Stubbed fetch returning a reference with no link, an empty body, and a 429.
steps: Query with a title-only reference and with an absent ai_overview block.
Query with a non-ok (429) response.
items.expected: A title-only reference still yields a citation and still matches by hostname (the Gemini redirect case); an empty body yields empty answer/citations; a 429 THROWS rather than recording an empty reading as a real observation.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — 2 tolerance cases passed.
items.notes: The throw matters: a silent empty reading would persist 'we looked and the practice was absent' when in fact we never got an answer.

items.title: Runner records one observation per prompt per configured engine
items.precondition: Inject a configured fake gemini adapter; the model's record() is mocked (suite is DB-mocked).
steps: Run the observation for a category+city.
Inspect the summary and the record() calls.
items.expected: Two prompts run, two observations recorded, capture_method carried through on every write, and the unconfigured engines reported as skipped rather than run.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — runner happy-path case passed.

items.title: A persistence failure PROPAGATES — never relabelled an engine failure (§3.2)
items.precondition: Mock AiVisibilityObservationModel.record() to reject.
steps: Inject a healthy fake engine and make every write reject.
Run the observation and inspect the rejection and the call count.
items.expected: The run REJECTS with the database error and aborts on the first failed write. A database outage is never reported as a successful run.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — 'PROPAGATES a persistence failure' passed. MUTATION-VERIFIED: collapsing the two boundaries back into one catch fails this test and nothing else.
items.notes: Dave review #165 round 2, item 1. Before the fix, one try/catch wrapped BOTH adapter.query() and record(), so a rejected write was logged as 'engine query failed', counted as an engine failure, and the run RESOLVED — reporting success while having stored nothing. The two boundaries are now separate: a provider failure is isolated (logged, skipped, run continues); a persistence failure is logged with context and re-thrown.

items.title: An engine failure is isolated — a throwing adapter never fails the run
items.precondition: Inject an adapter whose query() throws.
steps: Run the observation with the throwing adapter.
Inspect the summary and the record() calls.
items.expected: The engine is reported in failedEngines (not enginesRun), nothing is persisted for it, and the run still resolves — one flaky third party never costs the others.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — failure-isolation case passed.
items.notes: Deliberately the OPPOSITE disposition from T10: a third-party outage is isolated, our own database failing is not.

items.title: An ignored idempotent conflict counts as a DUPLICATE, never as a fresh observation
items.precondition: Mock record() to report false (every write hit an existing row for this run_date).
steps: Run the observation with every write conflicting.
Inspect observationsRecorded, duplicateObservations, observationsProcessed, enginesRun and failedEngines.
items.expected: observationsRecorded=0, duplicateObservations=2, observationsProcessed=2 — and the engine is still reported as RUN, not failed, because it answered.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — duplicate-count case passed; the run log shows observationsRecorded:0, duplicateObservations:2.
items.notes: Dave review #165 round 2, item 4. Before the fix the runner incremented observationsRecorded on every non-throwing write, so a same-day re-run restated the full set as freshly captured. Fixing the count alone would have introduced the opposite lie — an idempotent re-run reporting every engine as FAILED — so ran-vs-failed is now decided by completed READINGS, and the insert count is reported separately.

items.title: The run skips entirely when there is no category/city
items.precondition: Inject a configured adapter and run with an empty category.
steps: Run the observation with category ''.
Inspect promptsRun and the record() calls.
items.expected: promptsRun=0, nothing persisted, all engines reported skipped — no fabricated observation from a meaningless prompt.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — no-prompt case passed.

items.title: record() reports whether a row actually landed, over an ignore-conflict chain
items.precondition: Spy on the model's query builder; the suite is DB-mocked.
steps: Drive record() with the chain returning one row, and with it returning none.
Inspect the returned boolean and the built chain.
items.expected: true when a row comes back, false when none does; the chain is onConflict(location_id, prompt_key, engine, run_date).ignore().returning('id') — without returning('id') the caller cannot know an insert occurred.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — 2 record() cases passed. MUTATION-VERIFIED: hardcoding `return true` fails the conflict case, and dropping .returning('id') fails both. SELF-CAUGHT GAP: the first cut of this fix had no model-level test — every runner test mocks record() out, so hardcoding `return true` passed the whole suite. This item exists because that mutation went uncaught.
items.notes: Dave review #165 round 2, item 4. Corroborated offline by compiling the chain through knex's pg dialect with NO connection open, which emits: insert into "ai_visibility_observation" (...) on conflict ("location_id", "prompt_key", "engine", "run_date") do nothing returning "id" — the conflict target matches the unique index ai_visibility_observation_idem (migration 20260715120000, line 42) exactly. HONEST LIMIT: that is still only the SQL TEXT plus the boolean mapping. That PostgreSQL returns no row for an ignored conflict is documented behaviour I did not execute; it is verified as T16, which needs a live database.

items.title: The observation read is tenant-scoped — one org cannot read another's rows (§11.7)
items.precondition: A fake query builder that ACTUALLY applies the where-clause (narrows a seeded row set), not a spy that records arguments. The suite is DB-mocked.
steps: Seed 4 rows: org 7 has two at location 42, org 8 has one at location 42 (the newest), org 7 has one at location 99.
Call AiVisibilityObservationModel.listForLocation(7, 42) and inspect the returned ROWS.
Call listForLocation(999, 42) for an org that owns nothing there.
items.expected: Only org 7's two rows come back, ordered newest-first; org 8's row is absent even though it shares location_id 42 and is the newest (so a missing org filter would surface it FIRST). Org 999 gets an empty set. organizationId is a REQUIRED argument, not an optional filter a caller may forget.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — 2 behavioral cross-org cases passed. NON-VACUOUSNESS PROVEN: reverting the model's WHERE to { location_id: locationId } fails both with 'expected [ o8-SECRET, o7-a, o7-b ] to deeply equal [ o7-a, o7-b ]' — the fake surfaces the actual cross-tenant leak, not a mismatched argument.
items.notes: Dave review #165 round 1, items 1-2; round 3 REPLACED the original test. The round-1/2 version asserted the query SHAPE (expect(whereArg).toEqual({...})) and was worthless: its stub returned [] unconditionally, so it passed whether or not the filter ever excluded a row. The fake now implements knex's where-semantics over real rows, so the assertion is about which ROWS return. HONEST LIMIT: this proves the model's filtering against a fake, NOT PostgreSQL — no live DB in this suite. It would not catch a BaseModel-level bug that drops the WHERE at the driver layer. The read still has ZERO production callers.

items.title: Detector records IDENTITY, not resemblance — the whole fabrication class (round 4)
items.precondition: Pure detector over adversary fixtures. No DB, no provider, no network.
steps: Name resemblance — detect against a lowercase suffix ('Smile Dental group'), an ampersand continuation ('Smile Dental & Orthodontics'), a name-forming connector ('Smile Dental of Austin'), a hyphenated locality, a capitalized continuation ('Smile Dental Group'), a LEFT-extended longer name ('Bright Smile Dental'), a left extension mid-sentence, and an all-lowercase left extension ('the bright smile dental option').

Citation resemblance — detect against a third-party title carrying our domain in prose; a URL carrying our domain in its QUERY; a URL carrying our domain in its PATH; a bare-host title from an engine that cannot prove its titles; a declared-canonical title that is prose rather than a host; and a TLD-only practice domain ('com') against an unrelated .com host.
Diagnostics — detect an answer where a lookalike appears on an EARLIER line than the genuine mention, and read `position`.

The other direction (non-vacuity) — detect a standalone mention, a mention preceded by a lookalike, a numbered-list mention, a possessive, a comma clause, a real host citation, a real subdomain citation, a canonical bare-host title (the Gemini contract), and the practice's own longer name matched exactly.
items.expected: Every resemblance case records NOTHING (no mention, no citation, no fabricated citedSource). Every identity case still records. `position` points at the identity-verified line (3), never the lookalike's (1).
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — describe('detectAppearance — identity not resemblance (round-3 adversary)'), 46 cases (32 resemblance + 14 real-evidence), all passed in the 83-test A3 run (Node 22, vitest exit 0). NON-VACUITY PROVEN BY REVERT: restoring the detector Dave reviewed (7e15e6af) fails 36 of 83 A3 tests (vitest exit 1); restoring the fix returns 83/83 (exit 0).
items.notes: The class is 'a match decided by resemblance rather than identity'. Dave named 2 cases; the sweep of the branch found 6 more that were also live fabrications: (1) LEFT-extension was entirely unguarded, so 'Bright Smile Dental' recorded a mention for 'Smile Dental'; (2) URL hosts were regex-scraped from the raw URL STRING, so a domain in a query param or path recorded a citation; (3) the same for the URL path; (4) `position` used the raw unguarded regex and pointed at the lookalike's line; (5) a TLD-only/garbage practice domain ('com') matched EVERY host under that TLD via the suffix check — a mass-fabrication amplifier; (6) all-lowercase prose gave no boundary signal at all. The runner, model, registry and prompt builder were swept too: they match on IDs, not text, so the class is confined to appearanceDetector.ts.

items.title: Gemini is the ONLY engine allowed to prove a citation from a title (round 4)
items.precondition: @google/genai is module-mocked; no key, no network.
steps: Query the Gemini adapter against the documented grounding shape: a vertexaisearch REDIRECT uri plus a bare-domain title.
Assert the adapter marks the citation titleIsCanonicalHost: true.
Detect appearance and confirm the redirect URI's host is not ours, so only the canonical title can prove the citation.
Confirm the SerpApi adapter marks titleIsCanonicalHost: false, and that its bare-host title records cited=false.
items.expected: Gemini's title proves the citation (cited=true, citedSource='brightsmiles.com'), because Gemini's contract names each grounding chunk by bare domain. SerpApi's title — a page headline — proves nothing, even when it happens to read as a bare domain.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — describe('GeminiVisibilityAdapter — the ONLY engine that may prove a citation by title') passed, plus the SerpApi title-only case asserting cited=false.
items.notes: HONEST LIMIT: the client is mocked, so this proves the MAPPING against the documented grounding shape, NOT the live API — T18 (live Gemini) stays pending. Title authority is default-deny: an adapter that says nothing gets titleIsCanonicalHost=undefined and its titles can never prove a citation. The detector independently requires a canonical title to BE a bare host, so even a mis-declaring adapter cannot smuggle prose through. RESIDUAL: that Gemini's grounding title is always the bare source domain is a documented-shape assumption, unverified live.

items.title: Independent adversary round — the first fix was INCOMPLETE, and what closed it
items.precondition: Pure detector. A fresh agent, given no stake in the fix, tasked ONLY to refute it.
steps: Task an independent adversary to break the fixed detector empirically (run it, not read it).
Re-probe every CONFIRMED break it reported.
Fix the root cause, not the individual inputs.
Re-run both directions and the full gates.
items.expected: Every confirmed fabrication closes, every real-evidence case still records, and the absurd over-rejections are repaired.
items.evidence: All confirmed breaks re-probed and now record NOTHING: generic prose naming nobody ('Family dental practices in Austin are plentiful.' for identity 'Family Dental') → false; plurals ('Smile Dental clinics/groups/practices/centers/studios are common') → false; digit-leading prefixes ('1st Family Dental', '32 Smile Dental', '3D Smile Dental') → false; hyphen/em-dash/markdown/zero-width left extensions → false; common-phrase reuse ('Perfect smile outcomes vary' for identity 'Perfect Smile') → false; root-relative URL '/smiledental.com/reviews' → cited false. Over-rejections repaired: 'Smile Dental P.C.' now matches ITSELF (was permanently undetectable), and a double-spaced stored name matches. Real evidence still records: plain mention, whole-name bold, numbered list, dash-bulleted list after another practice, ALL-CAPS heading, possessive, comma clause, lookalike-first, own longer name, position=3.
items.notes: HONEST RECORD: the adversary's verdict on the first fix was 'does NOT close the fabrication class — the citation side is near-airtight; the name side is broken.' Its structural diagnosis was correct and is why the fix changed shape: the name path ENUMERATED surface forms (a word list, a capital letter, a space) while the citation path PARSES and compares a structured host. A word list is beaten by adding 's'; a capital-letter test is beaten by a digit; a space test is beaten by a hyphen. The remedy was an identity signal a list cannot express — CASE-PATTERN AGREEMENT with the stored name (prose 'Family dental' is not the name 'Family Dental'), plus plural-tolerant suffixes, a digit-aware left guard, and lookaround-based matching. The adversary ALSO confirmed what held: it could not bypass titleIsCanonicalHost default-deny, nor break URL host parsing (userinfo @, query, path, fragment, homoglyph, superstring, ccTLD, 'com'). It reported two of its own hypotheses as wrong rather than dress them up.

items.title: An ignored conflict really returns no row in PostgreSQL
items.precondition: A live database with the ai_visibility_observation table migrated.
steps: Insert one observation and note the returned id.
Insert the SAME (location_id, prompt_key, engine, run_date) again.
Confirm the second call returns no row and the table still holds exactly one.
items.expected: The first insert returns an id (record() -> true); the second returns nothing (record() -> false) and adds no row, so the duplicate accounting in T12 rests on real database behaviour.
items.notes: HONESTLY NOT RUN — no live database was available and none was stood up for this work. T14 proves the chain and the mapping against a mocked builder; this proves PostgreSQL's actual ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING ... RETURNING behaviour, which a mock cannot establish. The unique index ai_visibility_observation_idem (migration 20260715120000, line 42) matches the onConflict columns exactly, which is a static check, not a run. Must NOT be marked pass until someone runs it against a real database.

items.title: The migration lands against the dev database
items.precondition: Merge to dev/dave and let the deploy run npm run db:migrate against the dev database.
steps: Run the migration against dev.
Confirm ai_visibility_observation exists with the unique index and both CHECK constraints.
Roll back and confirm the table is dropped cleanly.
items.expected: The table is created additively with no error, and down() reverses it.
items.notes: HONESTLY NOT RUN. This suite is DB-mocked and no database was available; no DDL from this migration has executed anywhere. Additive and reversible by inspection only.

items.title: A live Gemini observation run records a true reading
items.precondition: A live GEMINI_API_KEY and a caller that invokes runAiVisibilityObservation for an onboarded location.
steps: Trigger one observation run for a real location.
Compare the recorded mentioned/cited/citedSource against what Gemini actually answered.
items.expected: The observation matches the live answer, with capture_method 'api_grounded' — a grounded API reading, explicitly a proxy for what a user sees, never the exact AI Overview.
items.notes: HONESTLY NOT RUN, and NOT CURRENTLY TRIGGERABLE: A3 has no wired caller. Nothing in src/ imports runAiVisibilityObservation outside its own module and the test file — there is no agent, worker, route, or schedule for it. This is the post-merge dev smoke test named in the get-found chain merge brief; it needs wiring first. Every proof above is unit-level with injected seams or stubbed fetch; no live provider call has been made.

items.title: A live SerpApi AI Overview run parses the real payload
items.precondition: A SERPAPI_API_KEY, plus the ToS/procurement decision SerpApi requires.
steps: Trigger one observation with the SerpApi adapter configured.
Compare the parsed answer/citations against the raw SerpApi response.
items.expected: text_blocks and references parse as T6 asserts against the stubbed shape, with capture_method 'serp_scrape'.
items.notes: HONESTLY NOT RUN — no key exists, so this HTTP path has never executed against the live API. T6/T7/T8 stub fetch with the documented shape; if the live payload differs from the documented one, only this item would catch it. The adapter stays dormant until a key is present (the runner skips unconfigured engines), so merging costs nothing. SerpApi scrapes Google under its own ToS — a procurement call for Corey, not a merge step.

items.title: A live Perplexity Sonar run parses the real payload
items.precondition: A PERPLEXITY_API_KEY.
steps: Trigger one observation with the Perplexity adapter configured.
Compare the parsed answer/citations against the raw Sonar response.
items.expected: choices[0].message.content and the citations array parse into an answer plus URL citations, with capture_method 'api_proxy'.
items.notes: HONESTLY NOT RUN — no key exists; this HTTP path has never executed. Unlike SerpApi, the Perplexity adapter has NO stubbed-shape test either, so its parse is currently unproven at any level. It stays dormant until a key is present. Flagged as the weakest-covered adapter.

items.title: The repo gates are clean at this head
items.precondition: Node 22 (v22.23.1), npm ci, at the branch head.
steps: Run npx tsc --noEmit.
Run npx vitest run (full suite).
Run npm run check:conventions -- --strict.
items.expected: Zero type errors, a green suite, and zero structural backend violations.
items.evidence: tsc --noEmit exited 0 (no output). Full suite: 61 files, 539 tests passed (491 at the reviewed head), captured from vitest's OWN exit code, not a piped grep. check:conventions --strict exited 0 with 'CLEAR structural violations — backend (size + console + db): 0'; no ai-visibility file appears in any finding, advisory or otherwise.
items.notes: The §11.7 heuristic lists 102 models repo-wide; AiVisibilityObservationModel is NOT among them (it references organization_id). That heuristic is a backstop, not proof — the real tenant proof is T15/T22.

items.title: Every unscoped inherited BaseModel entry point is sealed on the A3 model (§11.7/§5.5)
items.precondition: BaseModel's ACTUAL static surface enumerated with the TypeScript checker (not from a remembered list), at the branch head under Node 22.
steps: Enumerate BaseModel's public statics via the TS type checker.
Call each sealed method with arguments and assert it rejects.
Remove one seal and re-run npx tsc --noEmit.
items.expected: All 9 table-touching statics (findById, findOne, findMany, create, createReturningId, updateById, deleteById, count, paginate) are sealed: 8 are COMPILE errors (TS2554) when called with arguments, and all 9 throw at runtime. Removing a seal fails tsc.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts — 9 seal cases passed. NON-VACUOUSNESS PROVEN: deleting the findById seal makes tsc FAIL with 'src/__tests__/ai-visibility.test.ts(585,7): error TS2578: Unused @ts-expect-error directive' (exit 2), so a seal cannot be silently removed — tsc covers this test file (verified via tsc --noEmit --listFiles).
items.notes: ENUMERATION FINDING: the checker reported 11 public statics, not the 9 on the review list — the extras are transaction() and beginTransaction(). Both are DELIBERATELY NOT sealed and this is the honest residual: they never touch tableName, they return a raw Knex handle that can query ANY table, and that handle is reachable through every other model, so sealing them on this one class would close nothing while breaking the sanctioned BaseModel transaction pattern. The breadth of a raw handle is a BaseModel-wide property this batch does not open; it is a candidate for a separate BaseModel-level decision, not an A3 fix. HONEST EXCEPTION: count() is RUNTIME-ONLY sealed — BaseModel.count() is callable with zero arguments, so the arity trick cannot fire and AiVisibilityObservationModel.count() still type-checks. Documented on the method; no @ts-expect-error on that test (one would itself fail tsc as TS2578).
